title: Te Whakahaumanu o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Revitalising the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Centered on the awa (stream) that flows through our college lands, named for the native kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks. This lesson connects to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (stories) of Tāne Mahuta's children and the whakataukī: 'Ko te wai te ora o ngā mea katoa - Water is the life giver of all things'. Students will explore how traditional knowledge of water guardianship can address modern pollution challenges in our local waterway.
learningObjectives: Whakamahi i ngā kupu Māori e hāngai ana ki te kaitiakitanga wai (e.g., waiora, mauri, wai Māori) i roto i ngā kōrerorero (Use Māori vocabulary related to water guardianship in discussions)
Tuhituhi he whakaaro mō te oranga o te awa, ki te reo Māori, mā te āta tirotiro i te taiao (Write reflections in te reo Māori about the stream's health through environmental observation)
Whakamārama i te hononga o ngā kōrero tuku iho a Ngāti Kahungunu ki te tiaki i te awa (Explain the connection between Ngāti Kahungunu ancestral stories and stream protection)
Whiriwhiri he rongoā Māori hei whakapai ake i te mauri o te wai (Collaborate to propose Māori solutions to improve water quality)

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Awa, He Kōrero - Walk, Stream, Story
activities.description: Guided hīkoi (walk) to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua (elder) to learn its traditional name, significance, and pūrākau. Students use te reo Māori to describe what they see (wai Māori/clear water, paru/pollution) and record kupu hou (new words).

activities.title: Te Kimi Āhua - Seeking the Signs
activities.description: In small groups, students conduct wai (water) testing using simple kits (pH, clarity) and traditional observation methods (tirotiro/look, rongo/listen, hōhonu/depth). They record findings and kupu in a 'Pukapuka Awa' (stream journal) using sentence structures like 'Kei te [action] te wai' (The water is [action/state]).

activities.title: He Kōrerorero mō te Mauri - Discussing the Life Force
activities.description: Using a 'Te Whare Tapa Wha' model adapted for the awa (taha wai/water health, taha tinana/physical, taha hinemoana/spiritual, taha whānau/community), students discuss in te reo Māori how pollution affects its mauri (life force). Sentence starters provided: 'Mēnā ka paru te wai, ka...' (If the water is polluted, then...).

activities.title: Te Whakatakoto Rongoā - Proposing Solutions
activities.description: Groups develop a 'Mahere Kaitiaki' (Guardianship Plan) using a template with te reo Māori prompts. They propose one practical action (e.g., planting harakeke/flax to filter runoff) and create a simple waiata or karakia to express their commitment, using relevant kupu.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Aunty Hine Smith) for pūrākau and correct pronunciation
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Wai Ora' water testing kits and te reo Māori guidance sheets
Book: 'Ngā Whakataukī o Ngāti Kahungunu' (Whakapapa Publishing) for relevant proverbs
Digital app: 'Kupu' by Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori for instant word lookup and pronunciation

tasks: Submit completed 'Pukapuka Awa' (stream journal) with at least 10 kupu hou and 5 sentences in te reo describing observations
Present group 'Mahere Kaitiaki' (Guardianship Plan) orally for 2 minutes, using at least 3 key terms correctly (e.g., waiora, mauri, kaitiaki)
Peer-assess another group's plan using a te reo Māori rubric focusing on clarity and cultural relevance

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Whakarongo - Listening (AO: Understand and respond to spoken Māori related to the environment)
Te Reo Māori: Kōrero - Speaking (AO: Use Māori to communicate about immediate environment and solutions)
Te Reo Māori: Tuhituhi - Writing (AO: Write simple texts in Māori about a local issue)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment (AO: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts sustainability)
Health and Physical Education: Healthy Communities and Environments (AO: Investigate community and environmental factors that affect wellbeing)
